title: Proverbs 14
subtitle: Wise Woman Builds; Each Heart's Own Bitterness; Fear of the LORD as Fortress

sections.header: Verses 1–18 — Wise Woman Builds; Each Heart's Bitterness; Way Seeming Right

heb.paragraph: The wise woman bānĕtâ — builds — her house (v. 1). The verb is the same used of God building (bānâ) Eve from Adam's rib and of building the temple. A household is constructed through daily acts of wisdom, and it can be demolished by folly. The feminine subject connects to Lady Wisdom's architectural imagery in chapters 8–9.

There is a way that seems yāšār — straight, right — to a person, but its end is the way of death (v. 12). The terrifying point is that subjective moral certainty is no guarantee of truth. Self-deception is possible precisely because the wrong path feels right. Only external wisdom (God's word, wise counsel) can correct the internal compass.

refs.note: Wisdom has built her house -- the wise woman of 14:1 enacts what Lady Wisdom does.
The way that seems right but leads to death anticipates the Sermon's broad road.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The first half of ch.14 moves through a series of observations about appearance and reality: things that look one way but are another. The empty barn that has no grain (v.4); the smooth-talking person who is not wise (v.7); the path that appears right but ends in death (v.12). Alter notes the appearance-reality theme is ch.14's structural interest — wisdom is the capacity to see through appearance to reality.

'Each heart knows its own bitterness, and no one else can share its joy.' This is one of Proverbs' most psychologically intimate proverbs. Alter notes the observation is about the limits of human empathy: there is a dimension of inner experience — both suffering and joy — that is fundamentally private. No external observer, however wise, can fully know another person's inner life.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The 'wise woman who builds her house' (v.1) is one of Proverbs' most compact portraits of wisdom in action. Waltke notes the woman as wisdom's embodiment in ch.14 anticipates the Eshet Chayil of ch.31 — both are women whose wisdom is expressed in household management, social care, and economic productivity. The foolish woman who tears her house down with her own hands is the counter-portrait.

'There is a way that appears to be right, but in the end it leads to death.' This proverb (repeated verbatim in 16:25) is Proverbs' most sobering epistemological warning. Waltke notes the danger is not the obviously wrong path but the path that appears right — that feels like wisdom, is internally justified, is socially approved, but ends in death. The fear of the LORD is the corrective because only divine wisdom can distinguish appearance from reality in the deepest matters.

Calvin: 'Where there are no oxen, the manger is clean — but much revenue comes by the strength of the ox. This proverb warns against the tidiness of non-effort: the barn that has no grain also has no mess. True productivity requires the acceptance of disorder, difficulty, and labour. The person who keeps their life neat by avoiding responsibility is keeping an empty barn.'

Calvin: 'Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a reproach to any people. This is the most important principle of political theology in Proverbs: the measure of a nation's greatness is not its military power, economic output, or cultural achievement, but its righteousness — its fidelity to the moral order that God has established.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'where there are no oxen the manger is empty, but from the strength of an ox come abundant harvests' discusses the agricultural economics: oxen were the primary agricultural power source for plowing. No oxen = no harvest, but also no messing the barn. The proverb teaches that productivity requires accepting the costs (mess, expense) of the tools needed to produce it.

The NET note on 'a way that seems right to a man' (derekh yashar lifney ish) discusses the subjective character of human moral reasoning — what seems right to the individual knower may be fatally wrong. The note traces the appearance-reality gap through Proverbs: the simple are led astray by appearances (9:17); the mocker mistakes his folly for wisdom (14:6). Only the fear of the LORD provides an external standard.

hist.context: The first half opens with the wise woman as builder (v.1). V.10 is an island of interiority: each heart knows its own bitterness. V.12 is the chapter's most sobering verse: self-perceived rightness is not a reliable guide.

sections.header: Verses 19–35 — Fear of the LORD: Fortress and Fountain; Kindness to the Poor Honours God

heb.paragraph: In the fear of the LORD one has strong confidence, and his children will have a maḥseh — a refuge (v. 26). The word is used elsewhere of God himself as refuge (Ps 46:1). The promise extends beyond the individual: a parent's reverence for God creates a shelter that protects the next generation.

Whoever oppresses the poor reproaches his Maker, but whoever is gracious (ḥônēn) to the needy honours God (v. 31). The logic is theological: the poor bear the image of God, so treatment of them is treatment of God. Ḥônēn (from ḥānan, to be gracious) is the same root used of God's own grace toward humanity.

refs.note: Fountain of life -- 14:27 applies the same image to the fear of the LORD.
Whatever you did for the least -- the NT fulfilment of v.31's identification of the poor with their Maker.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The poverty theology of vv.20–31 is the chapter's most challenging material. Alter notes the sequence: the poor are despised by their neighbours (v.20), which is then characterised as sin (v.21 explicitly), and ultimately as contempt for God (v.31). The progression is damning: social indifference to the poor begins as a fact of life (v.20) and ends as theological blasphemy (v.31). The proverbs force the reader to follow the logic all the way.

Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a reproach to any people' (v.34) is Proverbs' most politically comprehensive proverb. Alter notes the 'nation' (goy) is unusual — most Proverbs concerns individuals; this concerns peoples and nations. The political application of the wisdom principles is significant: the same moral order that governs personal life also governs communal and national life.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The second section of ch.14 develops the social and political dimensions of wisdom. Waltke notes the poor-and-rich cluster (vv.20–21, 31) brackets the section: the poor are despised by neighbours (v.20) but to oppress the poor is to insult their Maker (v.31). Between these poles, wisdom ethics about generosity, truth, and national righteousness are developed.

'Whoever oppresses the poor shows contempt for their Maker, but whoever is kind to the needy honours God.' This is Proverbs' most direct statement of the theological basis of poverty-ethics. Waltke notes the argument is not primarily social (the poor are human too) but theological: God is the Maker of the poor. To treat the poor contemptuously is to express contempt for God's creative act in making them.

Calvin: 'The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life, turning a person from the snares of death — this is the comprehensive benefit of the foundational virtue. All the specific wisdoms of the proverb collections flow from this single source: right orientation toward God produces right orientation toward everything else.'

Calvin on honouring God through the poor: 'Whatever honour or dishonour is done to the poor is done to God, since they are his creatures and bear his image. The rich who despise the poor are forgetting that God is their common Maker, and that the difference in their conditions is God's appointment, not nature's inevitability.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'the fear of the LORD is a fountain of life, turning a person from the snares of death' connects to 10:11 ('mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life') and 13:14 ('the teaching of the wise is a fountain of life'). The fountain-of-life image is applied to three things: the righteous person's speech, the wise teacher's instruction, and the fear of the LORD. All three are expressions of the same reality.

The NET note on 'righteousness exalts a nation' discusses the political-theological claim: tsedaqah (righteousness, covenant faithfulness) at the national level produces national exaltation (rum, lifting up). The note discusses whether this is a promise (faithfulness guarantees national prosperity) or an observation (faithful nations generally flourish). The note favours the latter, given Proverbs' general preference for observable principles over mechanical guarantees.

hist.context: The second half addresses poverty (vv.20-21,31), the fear of the LORD as fortress and fountain (vv.26-27), peace-heart versus envying-bones (v.30), and righteousness as national exaltation (v.34). The chapter's theological centre: how you treat the poor reveals your relationship to their Maker.

ppl.name: The wise woman
ppl.text: The constructive force in household and community.

ppl.name: The poor
ppl.text: Their oppression is contempt for the Maker.

ppl.name: The patient person
ppl.text: Patience is the practical face of wisdom.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Whoever oppresses the poor shows contempt for their Maker, but whoever is kind to the needy honors God."NoteThe most direct equation of social ethics and theology in ch.14. Kindness to the poor is a form of worship.

src.title: Fear of the LORD
src.quote: Vv.26-27: fortress and fountain. The double occurrence here is the chapter's theological heart.
src.note: The fear of the LORD is Proverbs' foundational category (1:7).

src.title: National righteousness
src.quote: Tsedaqah includes justice, equity, and social order. Ancient Near Eastern wisdom addressed the character of nations.
src.note: Proverbs has a social ethics dimension, not just personal virtue.

rec.text: Righteousness exalts a nation -- most cited Proverbs text in political theology, invoked by Augustine, Aquinas, and Calvin.
The way that seems right but leads to death -- biblical diagnosis of sincere but mistaken paths.

rows.label: The Wise Woman Builds
rows.text: The wise woman builds her house, but with her own hands the foolish one tears hers down. Wisdom is constructive; folly is self-destructive. The same two paths that run through all of Proverbs run through the domestic life of a household.

rows.label: The Fear of the LORD: Fountain of Life
rows.text: The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life, turning a person from the snares of death. The fear of the LORD that opens the book (1:7) appears throughout as the source from which all specific wisdom flows.

tx.title: Wisdom builds her house — singular/plural variants
tx.content: MT reads 'The wisest of women builds her house' (ḥakmôt nāšîm). The LXX reads 'wise women built houses' (σοφαὶ γυναῖκες ᾠκοδόμησαν οἴκους), pluralizing both subject and object. The Vulgate follows MT's singular. Targum Proverbs paraphrases: 'Wisdom has built the house of her teaching.'
tx.note: The MT's singular 'house' personifies Wisdom as in chapters 1–9; the LXX's plural domesticates the metaphor into practical advice about household management.

debate.title: Proverbs 14:34 and Political Theology: Can Nations Be 'Righteous'?

positions.name: Yes: nations are moral agents with covenant obligations
positions.argument: Nations can be and are evaluated by the moral standards of wisdom — not as covenant communities in the OT-Israel sense, but as corporate entities whose policies and cultures reflect righteousness or wickedness. Prov 14:34 applies the personal wisdom ethics to national life directly.

positions.name: The proverb applies to Israel specifically, not all nations
positions.argument: The political theology of this proverb is primarily addressed to Israel as a covenant nation under God's specific governance. Applying it directly to all nations requires the kind of general natural-law extension that is theologically defensible but goes beyond the verse's original context.

positions.name: Both personal and political application is valid
positions.argument: Proverbs 14:34's formulation is universal — 'any people' (leumim), not specifically Israel. The general wisdom-principle that moral faithfulness produces flourishing and wickedness produces reproach is a natural-law observation as much as a covenant promise, applicable to all human communities.

prayer_prompt: The wise woman builds her house, but the foolish one tears it down with her own hands. Consider asking God about what you're building or tearing down — often without realising it.

coaching.tip: 'The wise woman builds her house, but with her own hands the foolish one tears hers down' (v. 1). Women appear throughout Proverbs as builders or destroyers, wisdom or folly. This isn't gender essentialism but recognition that households rise or fall on daily decisions. Building is slow; destruction can be swift.

'In the fear of the LORD one has strong confidence, and his children will have a refuge' (v. 26). The benefits of wisdom extend to the next generation. Your fear of God becomes your children's shelter. Proverbs thinks multi-generationally — today's choices create tomorrow's inheritance.
